<div style="text-align: center;"><font color="#FFFFFF" style="background-color:#000000;" size=+3> TLP:CLEAR </font></div>


The objective of this document is to decribe known attacks on VCenter and means to detect them. It assumes you are fairly familiar with VCenter and ESXi infrastructure but deep knowledge of it isn't needed to understand this document as an architecture reminder is available bellow.

## Architecture reminder

A very simple schema of the Vcenter infrastructure would be the following, where a VCenter controls 2 different ESXi, with each ESXi managing 2 VMs :

![](data:image/webp;base64,)

The vCenter server is usually an IIS server managing the ESXi and each ESXi is more or less a Linux asset with a logging of VMWare infrastructure.

Multiple agents are used for communication between vCenter, ESXi and vSphere client, it is important to know the name of those agents as they will be encountered quite often in the logs :

![](data:image/png;base64,)

## Vcenter Functionnalities

### High Availability

In the schema High Availability (HA) is represented, which allows to migrate VMs between ESXi in a HA cluster in case of server failure for exemple.

This feature is often disabled by attackers that try to encrypt VMs as it may lock them. See resources about Qilin for more information.

### ExecInstalledOnly

ExecInstalledOnly is a feature on ESXi that tries to prevent ransomware execution on ESXi hosts. It is implemented in two different flavors :

- boot/kernel setting (disabled by default as of vSphere 8.0)
- runtime setting (new in vSphere 8.0)

It works by blocking binaries that do not come as part of a VIB package, however it is far from beeing perfect as :

- Runtime setting is easy to disable
- Event with boot/kernel setting, it does not take Python into account

### SSH / TSM SSH

ESXi can have SSH enabled independently of vCenter and TSM SSH, which is used when using ESXi shell. If ESXi shell is not used by the admins, it is recommended to create detections rules on it and disable it.

### Link with Active Directory

It is possible to connect your ESXi to your Active Directory to enable directly users to interact with it. However be carefull of one thing : The member of the group "ESX Admins" will directly be administrator to your ESXi.

The ESXi checks periodically for this group and this resulted in CVE-2024-37085 that was discovered 14 years later, even though it was instantly seen as a security hole ... [Authentification AD & ESXi : ESX Admins - Hypervisor.fr](https://www.hypervisor.fr/?p=2292)([Ransomware operators exploit ESXi hypervisor vulnerability for mass encryption | Microsoft Security Blog](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2024/07/29/ransomware-operators-exploit-esxi-hypervisor-vulnerability-for-mass-encryption/))

### SOAP API

It is less known but there is a SOAP API to manage ESXi hosts. It is quite obscure and doesn't seem documented enough, however it IS logged in hostd.log.

# Logs

Interesting ESXi logs are the following :

- /var/run/log/shell.log <- Executed Shell Commands & Events
- /var/run/log/auth.log <- Authentication Events
- /var/run/log/hostd.log <- ESXi Host & Agent Events
- /var/run/log/vpxa*(log ou gz) <- Informations about vpxa

Individual VM logs are in the following location :

- `/vmfs/volumes/<Id>/<folder_hierarchy>/<Machine name>/vmware.log

For vCenter logs, the following resource can be used : [Location of vCenter Server log files](https://knowledge.broadcom.com/external/article/312194/location-of-vcenter-server-log-files.html)


<div style="text-align: center;"><font color="#FFFFFF" style="background-color:#000000;" size=+3> TLP:CLEAR </font></div>

### Resources

The sources used for the creation of this article are the following :
- Python encryption of ESXi : [VMware ESXi Servers Encrypted by Lightning-Fast Python Script](https://threatpost.com/vmware-esxi-encrypted-python-script-ransomware/175374/)
- ExecInstalledOnly : [ESXi 8.0 and execInstalledOnly - The good, the bad and the ugly - Truesec](https://www.truesec.com/hub/blog/esxi-8-0-and-execinstalledonly-the-good-the-bad-and-the-ugly)
- SOAP API log : [https://detect.fyi/vmware-esxi-logging-detection-opportunities-4fb56411ec21](https://detect.fyi/vmware-esxi-logging-detection-opportunities-4fb56411ec21)
- DFIR4VSphere : [DFIR4Sphere: Investigating VMWare vSphere Virtualization Platform](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2EXtHr0NDPk)
- SSH Key changed : [Jackpotting ESXi Servers For Maximum Encryption](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qxPXxWMI2i4)
- Qilin ransomware methodology :[Qilin ransomware’s newest tactics: Widespread encryption by any means necessary | ThreatLocker Blog](https://www.threatlocker.com/blog/qilin-ransomwares-newest-tactics-widespread-encryption-by-any-means-necessary)
- vCenter log files location : [Location of vCenter Server log files](https://knowledge.broadcom.com/external/article/312194/location-of-vcenter-server-log-files.html)
